video_title: This Is What an AI Unwind Looks Like
gist: Dan Nathan and Guy Adami argue an AI-trade unwind is underway: memory/semis (Micron, SK Hynix) are cratering, the compute-scarcity bull thesis is breaking as XAI/SpaceX, Meta and others lease out 'excess' compute on short-term contracts, and China's cheaper open-weight models (DeepSeek, Alibaba, Moonshot) are closing the gap. They see margin/valuation risk in Google, Nvidia, Dell, Netflix and Intel, while flagging Apple's contrarian breakout and a building geopolitical oil premium.

insights.claim: Micron collapsed from an all-time high of $1,255 set around June 25 to a $800 low on Friday, and $800 also marks its prior May all-time high, making it the technical support level to watch.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: The prior all-time high at $800 is the line in the sand; a decisive break signals the memory selloff is structural rather than another routine dip.
insights.quote: a stock that today, and again, today is Friday, traded down to $800. So, so think about that move for a second.

insights.claim: The DRAM ETF grew to roughly $22 billion in AUM (over $25 billion at its highs), an unusually fast rise for a sector ETF, and it round-tripped from ~80 in early-to-mid June to a Friday low of 48.65 before bouncing to 53.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: Explosive sector-ETF asset growth into the top is a classic crowding signal; the sharp intraday reversal ('spike bottom') is a tell to watch for whether memory stabilizes or breaks down.
insights.quote: This ETF guy has 22 billion dollars under management at highs. I think it was over 25. I I can't remember the last time I have seen a sector ETF grow like this.

insights.claim: Seagate, Western Digital, Micron, Samsung and SK Hynix make up roughly 40-45% of the DRAM ETF, and the ETF's Friday action looked like it was attempting a spike bottom while SMH and SOXX were still down 2%.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: Concentration in five memory names means the ETF is a clean proxy for the memory trade; a confirmed spike bottom would be an early signal the group has washed out.

insights.claim: The hosts have seen roughly 8% daily up-and-down moves in memory names about three times a week for the last three to four weeks, reflecting extreme volatility and indecision in the group.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: 8% swings multiple times a week signal a leadership group losing its footing; heightened realized volatility often precedes a regime change in a crowded trade.

insights.claim: A Bloomberg report that Google is pushing out its latest Gemini coding model triggered a 5% straight-line drop and a roughly 10% two-day selloff in the $4 trillion stock, reversing the week's rotation into hyperscalers.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: A 10% two-day move in a $4T name on a product-delay headline shows how fragile hyperscaler sentiment is; model-cadence slippage is now a market-moving catalyst.
insights.quote: the stock sold off 5% in a straight line. Okay, as we're recording right now, the stock is off two and a half percent... It was about a 10% two-day sell-off.

insights.claim: Google's gross margins are expected to fall from 71% this year to about 65% next year, with earnings growth decelerating from 12% to 7%, largely because of hundreds of billions in cumulative capex funded partly by equity and debt raises.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: A ~6-point gross-margin compression driven by AI capex is the core bear case: the market may re-rate Google on a deteriorating margin structure even as revenue accelerates.
insights.quote: Gross margins are expected to go from this year's 71% to about 65%. Think about that. To 65%. Now, what's weighing on that? Capex is a big part of that

insights.claim: Google revenue growth is accelerating to 24% this year with slight deceleration to 22% next year, and the stock trades at about 23.5x both this and next year's earnings, reports Wednesday after the close, with a 7% implied earnings move.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: Accelerating revenue but compressing margins sets up a binary print; the 7% implied move plus a 23.5x multiple frames the risk/reward into Wednesday's report.

insights.claim: Guy Adami argues Google's valuation is still compelling and its search/YouTube moat intact after the pullback from a May all-time high of ~$408 (from a March low of $274) to ~$350, viewing the setup as favorable into earnings.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: The bull case rests on moat durability and a de-risked post-selloff setup; the debate between moat and margin dilution is the key swing factor into the print.

insights.claim: Google's TPUs are competing directly with Nvidia and taking clients, and Google now has five or six products with over two billion monthly active users, supporting the 'vertical AI' bull case built on top of search.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: TPU client wins are a direct threat to Nvidia's demand base and a differentiator for Google, but the open question is whether that distribution translates into profitable monetization.

insights.claim: Dan Nathan cites Ben Thompson's roughly two-year-old warning that AI could be Google's 'Kodak moment' — an existential threat to search — noting it hasn't materialized yet but the risk remains live as moats and valuations can change quickly.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: The 'Kodak moment' frame is a reminder that legacy-tech moats can erode fast; investors should treat Google's search dominance as contingent, not permanent.
insights.quote: there was a potential for Google to have sort of its Kodak moment and his point was the existential risk and how quickly the ground moves beneath the feet of these legacy companies

insights.claim: The core AI bull thesis of compute scarcity is being undermined as Meta and XAI/SpaceX start renting out 'excess' compute — Meta bounced on the news — contradicting the idea that GPUs and data-center capacity are scarce.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: If the biggest spenders have surplus compute to lease, the scarcity narrative underpinning AI-infrastructure valuations is false; this is a directly tradeable crack in the bull case.
insights.quote: Isn't the whole story in this space about scarcity? Right? And so like help me think about that

insights.claim: XAI was losing about $1 billion a month, and SpaceX's ~$250 billion property purchase only pencils because Anthropic and Google each agreed to lease its excess compute for about $1 billion just days before the IPO — but those are three-month, not long-term, contracts.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: The valuation depends on short-dated compute leases dressed up as durable demand; if those three-month contracts aren't renewed, the entire property/valuation math collapses.
insights.quote: the problem with those contracts is they were threemonth outs. Okay, we keep hearing about long-term agreements. That's the thing that sounds so durable... That's the opposite of that

insights.claim: SpaceX's stock, priced at $135 and briefly trading as high as ~$185-$225, has fallen to about $125 and traded very poorly post-IPO, which the hosts warn could weigh heavily on broader market sentiment for AI names.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: A high-profile mega-cap IPO breaking below its offer price is a sentiment contagion risk; poor aftermarket action can freeze the pipeline for smaller AI IPOs.

insights.claim: Trillion-dollar AI IPOs must trade well for smaller $10-15 billion mid-cap deals to get done, so SpaceX's weak debut signals a broader closing of the IPO exit window for private AI investors.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: A frozen exit path pressures private AI valuations and VC markups; watch mega-cap aftermarket performance as a leading indicator for the whole private-market cycle.

insights.claim: Guy Adami points to the Jim Chanos podcast as the succinct bear case, invoking 'watch the fine print' — Elon Musk's admission that the compute contracts are very short-term — as the detail that blows a hole in the scarcity story.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: Chanos-style skepticism reframes booked 'long-term agreements' as short-dated obligations; scrutinizing contract duration is the analytical edge in this AI cycle.
insights.quote: a man hears what he wants to hear and disregards the rest. And a lot of people until recently have been disregarding all the rest.

insights.claim: SK Hynix listed a $26.5 billion deal last week, adding fresh supply right in the middle of the DRAM/memory drawdown.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: A large new listing during a memory selloff adds supply and sentiment pressure to an already-crowded, declining group.

insights.claim: Per Torsten Slok of Apollo, the Epoch Capabilities Index shows open-weight (Chinese) models now trail closed-weight US frontier models by only about four months, with the gap steadily narrowing.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: A shrinking four-month capability gap undercuts the premium paid for US frontier labs' hundred-billion-dollar spend; commoditization risk is real and measurable.
insights.quote: the openweight models uh trail the closed weight frontier models... they're trailing about four months. This gap is getting less and less.

insights.claim: China is building competitive AI far more cheaply — DeepSeek raised $7 billion at a $52 billion valuation and Alibaba announced $50 billion of capex over three years — while US labs spend hundreds of billions, and China is doing it without Nvidia's most advanced chips.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: If China matches capability on a fraction of the capex and without top Nvidia chips, it pressures both US AI ROI assumptions and Nvidia's China demand thesis.
insights.quote: our companies are battling each other with trillions of dollars in capex and the Chinese are doing something very differently on much less.

insights.claim: Chinese customers are declining to buy Nvidia's advanced chips even after the president cleared Nvidia to sell into China, signaling China's deliberate pivot toward domestic, lower-cost AI development.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: Voluntary refusal of Nvidia chips despite export approval is a bearish demand signal for Nvidia and a bullish one for Chinese self-sufficiency.
insights.quote: the Chinese aren't buying them.

insights.claim: A Bloomberg headline that China's Moonshot unveiled an AI model narrowing the gap with US firms hit US AI names on Friday, weighing on Meta, Google, Amazon and Microsoft down the stack.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: Chinese model releases are now direct catalysts for US mega-cap AI weakness; competitive-parity headlines are a recurring downside trigger to monitor.
insights.quote: China's moonshot unveils AI model that narrows gap with US firms. So this is one of the reasons why we're seeing weakness today.

insights.claim: Apple broke out to the upside — the opposite of the rest of tech — on Chinese approval to work with Baidu and Alibaba, with money rotating out of semis and memory into Apple as it is now rewarded, rather than punished, for not spending on AI.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: Apple is trading as the defensive AI-capex haven; the narrative flip from 'punished for not spending' to 'rewarded for not spending' explains the rotation and may reverse if AI sentiment recovers.
insights.quote: there was a period of time where Apple is being punished for not spending. I think now they're being rewarded for not spending.

insights.claim: Apple trades at roughly 33.5-34x next year's earnings with expected earnings growth of only ~10% and sales growth of ~9%, a growth-company multiple on what is arguably now a value company, into an earnings release with its 200-day moving average near $275.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: A 34x multiple on high-single-digit growth is a valuation mismatch; the setup looks poor into earnings, making Apple a candidate to fade after its defensive run.
insights.quote: when Apple was a growth company, it was trading probably in the low teens... now that it's probably some sort of value company, it's trading at a growth company multiple. So, something's a skew here.

insights.claim: Apple's China iPhone sales reaccelerated more than 20% last quarter (China is ~20% of revenue), but memory-driven price increases plus a weak Chinese economy, a fall Siri-with-AI relaunch, and a lawsuit against OpenAI over departing hardware staff make Dan Nathan skeptical of the rally.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: If Apple must raise prices into a weak China amid product/AI execution issues, the reacceleration may not last; the breakout could be a rotation artifact rather than a fundamental turn.
insights.quote: This thing seems like a mess to me. I'm just going to be really like clear on the product front. And I just don't get the enthusiasm about it.

insights.claim: Apple's gross margin was ~47% last fiscal year and is expected to be ~48%, with a mix shift toward services and AI services seen as the main positive driver.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: Services-led margin expansion is the one clear bullish lever for Apple, but it is modest and may not justify a 34x multiple.

insights.claim: Nvidia trading at only ~18x next year's earnings (below a market multiple, in the high teens) is a warning, not a bargain, because the market is signaling it does not believe Nvidia's ~74% gross margins will last.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: A low multiple on peak margins is a value trap; the market is pricing margin erosion, so cheapness alone is not a buy signal for Nvidia.
insights.quote: Nvidia the point that I've been making it's either a very good thing that they're trading at 18 times the next year's numbers or a very bad thing. I obviously think it's the latter

insights.claim: Per Jim Chanos, Dell's gross margins have fallen from 22% to about 17.5% over the last year and a half and are likely to go lower if component pricing stays high, illustrating how AI hardware economics squeeze the server assemblers below the chipmaker.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: Component suppliers and assemblers absorb margin pain from high Nvidia/memory pricing; Chanos argues they shouldn't trade at a higher multiple than Nvidia itself.
insights.quote: So Dell's gross margins have gone from 22% down to about 17 and a half% over the last year and a half. And they're probably going to go lower

insights.claim: Dell topped out around 470 and now trades sub-400, at roughly 21.5x this year and 18x next — a premium to Nvidia — and has become a crowded name partly because the president has repeatedly mentioned it, a tailwind that could reverse.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: Dell paying a premium to Nvidia on shrinking margins is a relative-value short setup; political attention adds a two-sided catalyst risk.

insights.claim: Guy Adami's overarching warning is not to be seduced by AI hardware valuations that look cheap, because Nvidia's margins will inevitably start to decrease regardless of what Jensen Huang says, and cheap multiples can quickly look expensive.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: On peak-margin, peak-cycle names a low multiple is a trap; investors should discount forward margins rather than trust management's durability claims.
insights.quote: don't be seduced by valuations that seem um interesting because... they can seem very expensive, especially with a name like Nvidia where there's just an inevitability that margins are going to start to decrease

insights.claim: Netflix fell 9% because free cash flow came in around $1.54 billion versus the ~$2.75 billion expected, signaling it is spending far more than the market wants on content and buying growth despite in-line EPS and sales.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: The free-cash-flow miss, not the P&L, is what broke the stock; content-spend intensity and lack of organic growth are the metrics that now matter for Netflix.
insights.quote: free cash flow is expected to be $2.75 billion. It came in I believe at 1.54 something like that pretty significant

insights.claim: Netflix has round-tripped back to its 2021 all-time high near $68 (after a prior run to ~$135), but with the earnings gap now below that level there is no technical support, making it a 'no touch' despite the flush.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: With support broken and a gap below, Netflix has no clear technical floor; a two-to-three-times-normal-volume flush day is the only reason to even look at it.
insights.quote: if I draw a horizontal line from that 2021 high... the gap is actually below that... So, there's no there's really no support from technical standpoint.

insights.claim: WTI crude at ~$81.25 (Brent ~$86.50) has bounced ~22% off a multi-month low near $67.50, but Guy Adami says the bounce is not commensurate with tight fundamentals — SPR and gasoline reserves are down to 'tag ends' and nobody is talking about it.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: Depleted strategic and gasoline reserves are an underappreciated bullish supply backdrop; the equities are supported anywhere in this crude range even without a strong price view.
insights.quote: we are down to tag ends in terms of the SPR, in terms of gasoline reserves, it's not good. Nobody's talking about it.

insights.claim: Guy Adami is bullish refiners and energy equities — Valero made an all-time high Friday, alongside strength in PSX and Marathon Petroleum, and he expects Exxon's downtrend since March to break to the upside, with ConocoPhillips, Chevron and OIH also buys unless crude collapses.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: The trade is long energy equities rather than the commodity; refiner crack-spread dynamics and a supportive crude range keep these names working absent a crude crash.
insights.quote: unless you think crude is going to collapse which I don't think you got to stay low these equities Dan in my opinion.

insights.claim: With the Middle East ceasefire over and bombing accelerating for six days, Guy Adami expects a geopolitical premium to build into oil, even though the VIX (at 18), gold and the bond market are not yet pricing that risk.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: A disconnect between rising geopolitical risk and complacent VIX/gold/bond markets is a potential asymmetric setup to hedge or position for a premium re-rating.
insights.quote: even with the VIX up today at 18 does not take that into consideration nor does the gold market and by the way neither does the bond market.

insights.claim: The hosts frame the Middle East conflict as a 'calendar game' tied to the November 4 midterm election, with adversaries trying to wait out the administration while the US tries to bomb them into submission, and warn winning may require boots on the ground into the fall.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: An election-timed conflict raises the odds of escalation into the fall; markets pricing no premium leaves oil and volatility exposed to headline risk.
insights.quote: it's crazy to think that the IRGC and these folks are really they probably have a calendar up and they're like they looking at election day on November 4th

insights.claim: Intel has fallen 35% from an all-time high of $142 on June 30 to $93 in under a month, with theoretical technical support near its prior $80 post-earnings gap and the 200-day moving average around $64, but Guy Adami says the valuation makes zero sense and doubts Thursday's report changes that.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: Despite a violent 35% drop into technical support, the fundamental valuation concern remains, so bounces into $80/$64 are levels to fade rather than buy.
insights.quote: the stock has gone from 142 down to 93 bucks. It's a pretty significant move in a very short period of time... the valuation makes zero sense.

insights.claim: Capital One (Tuesday earnings) is the key read on the lower-K consumer, and Guy Adami expects rising delinquencies across credit cards, student loans, autos and mortgages to force commentary the market won't like, in contrast to the upper-K American Express consumer.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: Capital One is a proxy for consumer credit stress; a K-shaped-economy read with worsening delinquencies is a macro warning even though its valuation and uptrend still look reasonable.
insights.quote: if what I'm reading is true in terms of credit card delinquency rates, in terms of student loans, autos, even mortgage delinquencies, there's no way they're going to say anything that the market's going to like

insights.claim: Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley gave back all of their gains after making all-time highs following bank earnings, a sign the post-earnings enthusiasm in financials faded quickly.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: Bank leaders reversing all-time-high breakouts suggests broad risk appetite is deteriorating even outside the AI complex.
